data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-28T01:38:15.983Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically regarding synchronous LINQ operations.

Let me think about what I know about this topic:

In EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider, there has been a long-running effort to remove synchronous APIs because the underlying Azure Cosmos DB SDK is async-only. The sync methods were implemented as "sync-over-async" (blocking on async calls), which is problematic.

Key timeline:
- **EF Core 9 (.NET 9)**: The Cosmos DB provider started throwing by default when sync APIs (like `ToList()`, `First()`, `SaveChanges()`, etc.) were called. You could opt back in to sync-over-async behavior.


- **EF Core 10 (.NET 10)**: The sync-over-async opt-in remained available but was deprecated.
- **EF Core 11 (.NET 11)**: The sync-over-async support is completely removed—the opt-in no longer works, and all synchronous LINQ operations on the Cosmos DB provider will throw `InvalidOperationException`.

So the user's code that was relying on the opt-in configuration in .NET 10 will break in .NET 11 because that escape hatch is gone. Following the established pattern Microsoft uses with EF Core, .NET 10 likely kept the opt-in available but deprecated it, and .NET 11 removed it entirely—which explains why their configuration no longer works. The solution is straightforward: convert all the synchronous database calls to their async equivalents and make the containing methods async as well.
